rationale: The citing paper does more than list this source in background references: it explicitly discusses Thomas and Morwitz (2009) as the ease-of-computation effect and summarizes its core finding about perceived price differences. That prior result is used to motivate the broader claim that numerical judgments are systematically malleable, which the 2024 paper then builds on in its own theorizing and contribution framing. The citation is therefore functionally important for conceptual positioning, even though the authors do not directly reuse the original method.
relation_description: The 2024 paper uses Thomas and Morwitz (2009) as a key prior demonstration that subjective numerical-difference judgments can be biased by processing fluency. It extends that line by introducing psychological distance as a new antecedent that changes the weighting of left versus right digits in numerical comparisons.
